After leaving parliament, Purnell was chair of [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research") from 2010 to 2012[[1]](#cite_note-1) before joining the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") as Director of Strategy from 2013 to 2016 and Director of Radio and Education from 2016 to 2020.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Sweney-3) In 2020 he was appointed vice-chancellor of [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London")[[4]](#cite_note-telegraph-20201023-4) before leaving in 2024 to become CEO of Flint, a British international advisory business.

**James Mark Dakin Purnell** (born 2 March 1970) is a British former [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") politician who served as the [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") for [Stalybridge and Hyde](/wiki/Stalybridge_and_Hyde_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stalybridge and Hyde (UK Parliament constituency)") from 2001 to 2010 and also as a Cabinet minister from 2007 to 2009. After leaving parliament, Purnell was chair of [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research") from 2010 to 2012[[1]](#cite_note-1) before joining the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") as Director of Strategy from 2013 to 2016 and Director of Radio and Education from 2016 to 2020.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Sweney-3) In 2020 he was appointed vice-chancellor of [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London")[[4]](#cite_note-telegraph-20201023-4) before leaving in 2024 to become CEO of Flint, a British international advisory business.

Purnell served as [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport") from 2007 to 2008 and [Secretary of State for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Work_and_Pensions "Secretary of State for Work and Pensions") from 2008 to 2009. He resigned from the Government on 4 June 2009, criticising the leadership of [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Purnell was born in the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"); he received most of his education in France before returning to study for his [A-Levels](/wiki/A-Level "A-Level") at the [Royal Grammar School, Guildford](/wiki/Royal_Grammar_School,_Guildford "Royal Grammar School, Guildford"), and then studied [Philosophy, Politics and Economics](/wiki/Philosophy,_Politics_and_Economics "Philosophy, Politics and Economics") at [Balliol College, Oxford](/wiki/Balliol_College,_Oxford "Balliol College, Oxford"). While a student, he worked during summer vacations as a researcher for [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") between 1989 and 1992. After graduating from Oxford with a [first class degree](/wiki/British_undergraduate_degree_classification "British undergraduate degree classification"), he worked as a research fellow at the [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research") before moving to the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") to become its Head of Corporate Planning.

Between May 1994 and October 1995, he was a Labour councillor in Islington, representing Canonbury East ward. In 1997, Purnell returned to work as a special adviser[[5]](#cite_note-5) at [10 Downing Street](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street"), remaining in the post until 2001. He has also served as a consultant at Hydra Associates and as a board member of the [Young Vic](/wiki/Young_Vic "Young Vic") theatre as well as of the [Royal National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") and the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute"), and as a senior advisor to [Boston Consulting Group](/wiki/Boston_Consulting_Group "Boston Consulting Group").

## Member of Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Member of Parliament")]

Purnell was selected as the Labour candidate for the constituency of [Stalybridge and Hyde](/wiki/Stalybridge_and_Hyde_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stalybridge and Hyde (UK Parliament constituency)") in 2001, and won the seat in [that year's general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") with a majority of 8,859. While a Labour MP, he was a member of the [Work and Pensions Select Committee](/wiki/Work_and_Pensions_Select_Committee "Work and Pensions Select Committee") in the House of Commons from 2001 to 2003, the Chair of the All-Party Group on Private Equity and Venture Capital between 2002 and 2003, and the Chair of [Labour Friends of Israel](/wiki/Labour_Friends_of_Israel "Labour Friends of Israel") from 2002 to 2004.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### In government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/James_Purnell_Ministerial_portrait.jpg/250px-James_Purnell_Ministerial_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Purnell_Ministerial_portrait.jpg)

Purnell in 2009

In 2003, Purnell became [Parliamentary Private Secretary](/wiki/Parliamentary_Private_Secretary "Parliamentary Private Secretary") (PPS) to [Ruth Kelly](/wiki/Ruth_Kelly "Ruth Kelly") in the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office"), and in December 2004 he joined the Government as a [Whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)") in the government reshuffle following the resignation of [David Blunkett](/wiki/David_Blunkett "David Blunkett").

After Labour was returned to power in the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), he was appointed to the position of [Parliamentary under-secretary of state](/wiki/Parliamentary_under-secretary_of_state "Parliamentary under-secretary of state") for Creative Industries and Tourism in the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport"), where he was in charge of preparing the legislation that liberalised the alcohol licensing laws of England and Wales and created tax breaks for the film industry. In May 2006, he was promoted to be [Minister of State](/wiki/Minister_of_State "Minister of State") for Pensions in the [Department for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Department_for_Work_and_Pensions "Department for Work and Pensions"), replacing [Stephen Timms](/wiki/Stephen_Timms "Stephen Timms").

In 2007, he was named Consumer Champion of the Year by *[Which?](/wiki/Which%3F "Which?")* magazine for his work on pensions. *Which?* cited his "commitment to consumers in the development of the national pensions saving scheme", particularly for listening to stakeholders and for his contributions to the personal accounts for low and middle earners.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In June 2007, Purnell entered the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") as the [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport"); he was its youngest member. He was promoted to [Work and Pensions Secretary](/wiki/Work_and_Pensions_Secretary "Work and Pensions Secretary") after the resignation of [Peter Hain](/wiki/Peter_Hain "Peter Hain") on 24 January 2008.

In September 2007, a [Tameside General Hospital](/wiki/Tameside_General_Hospital "Tameside General Hospital") press release for a PFI deal in September 2007 included a photograph with an image of Purnell digitally inserted, which Purnell denied having to agreed to.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) In December 2008, Purnell proposed charging interest on crisis loans to the unemployed and pensioners made by the [Department for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Department_for_Work_and_Pensions "Department for Work and Pensions"), which were interest-free, at a rate of up to 26.8% per annum. This was met with great hostility and was blocked by the intervention of Gordon Brown.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

During the [2009 parliamentary expenses scandal](/wiki/United_Kingdom_parliamentary_expenses_scandal "United Kingdom parliamentary expenses scandal") led by *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, Purnell received public scrutiny regarding his "second home" arrangements and capital gains tax, however he was not found to have breached any rules and was not required to repay any monies.[[12]](#cite_note-Watt-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Resignation from the Cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Resignation from the Cabinet")]

On 4 June 2009, Purnell announced his resignation from [the Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom"), and called upon [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") to resign as Prime Minister.[[14]](#cite_note-14) His resignation came only days after the resignations of [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary") [Jacqui Smith](/wiki/Jacqui_Smith "Jacqui Smith"), whose expenses had been the subject of negative comments, and [Communities Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Housing,_Communities_and_Local_Government "Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government") [Hazel Blears](/wiki/Hazel_Blears "Hazel Blears"), who had also avoided paying capital gains tax on her property. The news came just minutes after polls closed in the [local](/wiki/2009_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2009 United Kingdom local elections") and [European elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), in which Labour performed badly.
His letter to the Prime Minister, which was also sent to *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")* and *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, the latter printing the letter in full on the front page.[[15]](#cite_note-resignation_letter-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

During 2010, Purnell was working on a project with Demos. He did not seek re-election as an MP at the [2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election") held in May 2010.[[17]](#cite_note-bbc19feb10-17)

## Post-parliamentary career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-parliamentary career")]

After leaving parliament, Purnell became the chair of the [Institute for Public Policy Research](/wiki/Institute_for_Public_Policy_Research "Institute for Public Policy Research"). It was touted that he would stand for the Labour candidacy to become [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London"), but he decided against this option.[[18]](#cite_note-18) He supported [David Miliband](/wiki/David_Miliband "David Miliband") in the [2010 Labour leadership election](/wiki/2010_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2010 Labour Party leadership election (UK)") and worked for his campaign, although he was subsequently offered the job of chief of staff to the new leader of the Labour Party, [Ed Miliband](/wiki/Ed_Miliband "Ed Miliband"), a job he turned down.

Purnell became linked with the [Blue Labour](/wiki/Blue_Labour "Blue Labour") movement within the Labour Party[[19]](#cite_note-19) and in April 2011 he was appointed by the Boston Consulting Group as a Special Advisor to their Public Sector Group.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In July 2011, he appeared on *[Newsnight](/wiki/Newsnight "Newsnight")* with proposals for welfare reforms, as part of his involvement in Blue Labour. He called for a National Salary Insurance, a [job guarantee](/wiki/Job_guarantee "Job guarantee") and free universal childcare, but also said that "freebies" such as [Winter Fuel Allowance](/wiki/Fuel_poverty_in_the_United_Kingdom#Excess_winter_deaths "Fuel poverty in the United Kingdom") and [free bus passes](/wiki/Concessionary_Bus_Travel_Act_2007 "Concessionary Bus Travel Act 2007") should not become sacred. He did not rule out returning to [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in 2015, but declared his support for [Ed Miliband](/wiki/Ed_Miliband "Ed Miliband") and his leadership.

Purnell was also a film producer at Rare Day, who produced the docuementary film *One Mile Away* in 2013.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### BBC career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: BBC career")]

In February 2013 Purnell left the IPPR in order to rejoin the BBC as its Director of Strategy.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) As a senior BBC employee he had to resign his membership of the Labour Party.

An Employment Tribunal for unfair dismissal was brought by the BBC's former Chief Technology Officer, John Linwood, when he lost his job in 2013 after the Corporation's £100m [Digital Media Initiative](/wiki/Digital_Media_Initiative "Digital Media Initiative") failed. During the case an email from Purnell was cited in evidence, which read: "We need a clear line on [John Linwood] on whether he is resigning or being fired and why". The tribunal's response to this was: "It was notable that there was no third option in Purnell's mind, such as a different disciplinary outcome." The tribunal found the BBC's processes to have given an "apparently cavalier disregard for any of the accepted norms of a fair disciplinary process", and that there was a "deeply ingrained cultural expectation within the organisation of sacrificial accountability". Linwood was awarded £80,000 in damages, and it was later revealed that the BBC had spent £498,000 defending the claim.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In late September 2016, Purnell was appointed as the BBC's Director of Radio and Education, in succession to [Helen Boaden](/wiki/Helen_Boaden "Helen Boaden").[[26]](#cite_note-26) He took up his new position in late October 2016.[[3]](#cite_note-Sweney-3) *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* described a key facet of his role as attracting younger listeners and viewers to the BBC.[[4]](#cite_note-telegraph-20201023-4) In September 2020 the new director-general [Tim Davie](/wiki/Tim_Davie "Tim Davie") removed Purnell from his new smaller executive board.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### University of the Arts London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: University of the Arts London")]

Purnell left the BBC on 2020 to become president and vice-chancellor of [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London") in spring 2021.[[4]](#cite_note-telegraph-20201023-4)

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Purnell&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Later career")]

Purnell left the [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London") in 2024 and was appointed as CEO of Flint, a British international advisory business.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
